title: Step 7 – Share the Project Initiation Document
overview: Distribute the PID to the entire team, onboard new members, obtain client sign‑off, and use it as a reference for future check‑ins.
detailed_summary: The final step is to disseminate the completed PID to all relevant stakeholders. The document should be shared with the internal team, any new members joining later, and the client for formal sign‑off, which captures agreed assumptions, RACI assignments, and other critical information. Making the PID easily accessible supports ongoing check‑ins, helps keep the project on track, and serves as a reference point when new stakeholders are introduced or when approval processes become more complex than anticipated.
content: It’s done, you’ve created a comprehensive Project Initiation Document that sets your team up for success. Make sure to share it with the entire team and to new team members that are joining later on. It’s also great for check‑ins, later on, to ensure the team doesn’t lose track. Have the client sign off on it as well to formalize things like assumptions and RACI. It’ll come in handy to point to this document when new stakeholders get involved or when approval process turns out to be more complicated than anticipated.
search_queries: how to share a project initiation document
onboarding new team members with PID
common_questions: Who needs to receive the PID?
Do I need client sign‑off on the PID?
How should I store the PID for easy access?
Can the PID be used for later project reviews?
can_answer: Do I need client sign‑off on a PID?
best_practices: Store the PID in a shared, version‑controlled location.
Notify stakeholders via email with a link to the PID.
warnings: Never keep the PID in a personal folder only; team members may lose access.
